content.text: Tool invocation is the runtime counterpart of the tool schema. When the model selects a tool, it returns a
block in the response; the application executes the corresponding function and sends the result back as a
block. This exchange is the core of every function-calling interaction.
Tool invocation is a protocol, not a library call.

The model produces a structured block that your code must handle: execute the function, package the result, and send it back in exactly the format the API expects. Getting the message structure right is the critical implementation detail.
When the model decides to call a tool,
blocks. Each block carries three fields:
Unique block ID, e.g. 'toulu_01AbcXyz...'. Must be echoed in tool_result.
The tool name from the definition — use to route to the correct handler.
Keyword arguments to pass to the implementation function.
After executing the tool, append two messages: the assistant's tool_use message, then a user message containing
blocks. The API requires this pairing — every
must be answered before the conversation continues:

# After getting a tool_use response, build the tool_result turn

# 1. Append the assistant message (preserves tool_use blocks)
messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

# 2. Execute the tool and build a tool_result block
tool_results = []
for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "tool_use":
        if block.name == "get_current_time":
            result = get_current_time()
        else:
            result = json.dumps({"error": f"Unknown tool: {block.name}"})

        tool_results.append({
            "type": "tool_result",
            "tool_use_id": block.id,    # must match the tool_use id exactly
            "content": result            # string (JSON or plain text)
        })

# 3. Append the user message with results
messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

# 4. Get Claude's final response
final = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=512,
    tools=tools,
    messages=messages
)
print(final.stop_reason)    # "end_turn"
print(final.content[0].text)  # "The current time is 2024-03-15T10:32:44+00:00."
field is the link between the call and the result. An ID mismatch causes an API error. The
field is always a string — serialize dicts and lists with

blocks in a single response when it determines the tools can run concurrently — for example, fetching stock price and company info at the same time. Your loop must collect all results into a single user message:
in the list maps to exactly one
. The order of results does not need to match the order of calls — the IDs are the association.
When a tool raises an exception or returns a logical error, set
in the tool_result. The model reads this flag and handles the failure gracefully rather than treating it as successful output:
is_error: True causes Claude to explain the error to the user rather than try to interpret the failure string as data
Always catch exceptions inside the dispatch layer — an unhandled exception aborts the loop
Return a human-readable error message in content, not a Python traceback
Consider retrying transient errors (network timeouts) before returning is_error: True
The full pattern combines all of the above into a bounded loop. The
guard prevents infinite loops in case the model repeatedly requests tools without reaching a conclusion:
This pattern handles parallel tool calls, unknown tools, implementation exceptions, and multi-turn exchanges. It returns the model's final text when
Forgetting to append the assistant message before tool_result
API error: messages out of order
Always append assistant content before the tool_result user message
Sending tool_result as the assistant role instead of user
tool_result blocks go inside a role: user message
Copy block.id verbatim, never construct IDs manually
Returning a dict as content instead of a string
json.dumps() all non-string results before placing in content
Infinite loop if model keeps calling tools
Limit turns; return an error or partial answer if reached
, tool input arrives as a sequence of
events. Accumulate the partial JSON strings until the
event signals the block is complete before parsing:
Never parse partial JSON mid-stream — an incomplete
sequence is not valid JSON. Wait for
External tools — APIs, databases, file I/O — can hang indefinitely. Without a timeout guard, a stalled tool call stalls the entire agent loop. Use
on Unix to bound execution time:
as a cross-platform alternative. Return a timeout error as
so the model can report it gracefully.

) are transient and worth retrying. Non-transient errors (bad input, permission denied) should fail immediately. Wrap the dispatch in an exponential-backoff retry for connection-class exceptions only:
Retry only connection-class errors — retrying bad input wastes turns
Limit retries to 2-3; more than that indicates a service outage
Use jitter (add random 0-500ms) in production to avoid thundering herds
Log each retry attempt so you can distinguish flakiness from real failures

Tool invocations are the most diagnostically valuable events in an agent run. Logging tool name, input, result, and duration lets you trace exactly what the agent did and why an interaction went wrong:

import logging, time, json

logger = logging.getLogger("tools")

def dispatch_with_logging(block, registry: dict) -> dict:
    start = time.monotonic()
    logger.info("tool_call start  name=%s input=%s", block.name, json.dumps(block.input))

    result = dispatch_with_retry(block, registry)

    elapsed_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
    if result.get("is_error"):
        logger.warning(
            "tool_call error  name=%s duration_ms=%.1f error=%s",
            block.name, elapsed_ms, result["content"]
        )
    else:
        logger.info(
            "tool_call done   name=%s duration_ms=%.1f",
            block.name, elapsed_ms
        )
    return result

Structured logs (key=value pairs on a single line) are machine-parseable by log aggregators. At minimum log: tool name, input JSON, success/error, and duration. In production, route these logs to your observability platform (Datadog, CloudWatch, etc.) and alert on high error rates per tool name.
Tool invocation is a two-step exchange. The model returns
. The application executes the matching function, serialises the result to a string, and sends it back as a
matching the original call — inside a
message. When the model issues multiple
blocks, all results go in a single user message. Errors are signalled with
